    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Combinatorics.Additive.Basis
    19→
    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 1147
    22→
    23→Is the set of natural numbers $n \ge 1$ with $\lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < 1 / \log n$ an additive
    24→basis of order $2$ for every irrational $\alpha > 0$? Disproved by Konieczny.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1147](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1147)
    27→
    28→[Va99] Vaughan, R.C., *The Hardy-Littlewood method*, 2nd edition, Cambridge University Press,
    29→1997.
    30→
    31→[Ko16b] Konieczny, J., *Sets of recurrence as bases for the positive integers*. Acta Arithmetica
    32→(2016), 309–338.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Set Filter
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos1147
    38→
    39→/-- The distance of a real number from the nearest integer. -/
    40→noncomputable def distNearestInt (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    41→  min (Int.fract x) (1 - Int.fract x)
    42→
    43→/-- The set $A(\alpha) = \{ n \ge 1 : \lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < 1 / \log n \}$. -/
    44→noncomputable def setA (α : ℝ) : Set ℕ :=
    45→  {n : ℕ | n ≥ 1 ∧ distNearestInt (α * (↑n) ^ 2) < 1 / Real.log (↑n)}
    46→
    47→/-- The set $A(\alpha, \varepsilon) = \{ n \ge 1 : \lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < \varepsilon(n) \}$
    48→for a general threshold function $\varepsilon$. -/
    49→noncomputable def setAGen (α : ℝ) (ε : ℕ → ℝ) : Set ℕ :=
    50→  {n : ℕ | n ≥ 1 ∧ distNearestInt (α * (↑n) ^ 2) < ε n}
    51→
    52→/--
    53→Erdős Problem 1147 [Va99, 1.21]:
    54→
    55→Let $\alpha > 0$ be an irrational number. Is the set
    56→$$A = \{ n \ge 1 : \lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < 1 / \log n \},$$
    57→where $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ denotes the distance to the nearest integer, an additive basis
    58→of order $2$?
    59→
    60→This was disproved by Konieczny [Ko16b]. In particular, for $\alpha = \sqrt{2}$,
    61→the set $A$ is not an additive basis of order $2$.
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    64→theorem erdos_1147 : answer(False) ↔
    65→    ∀ α : ℝ, Irrational α → α > 0 →
    66→      (setA α).IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→/--
    70→Generalization of Erdős Problem 1147 [Ko16b]:
    71→
    72→For any function $\varepsilon(n) \to 0$, the set
    73→$A = \{ n \ge 1 : \lVert \alpha n^2 \rVert < \varepsilon(n) \}$ is not an additive basis
    74→of order $2$ for almost every $\alpha > 0$.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    77→theorem erdos_1147_general (ε : ℕ → ℝ) (hε : Tendsto (fun n => ε n) atTop (nhds 0)) :
    78→    ∀ᵐ α : ℝ, α > 0 → ¬(setAGen α ε).IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder 2 := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→end Erdos1147
    82→

     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1149
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1149](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1149)
    23→
    24→[Va99] Vardi, I., *Computational Recreations in Mathematica* (1999), Problem 1.34.
    25→
    26→[BeRi17] Bergelson, V. and Richter, F. K., *Dynamical generalizations of the prime
    27→number theorem and disjointness of additive and multiplicative semigroup actions* (2017).
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open Finset Filter Real Classical
    31→
    32→open scoped Topology
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1149
    35→
    36→/--
    37→Erdős Problem 1149 [Va99, 1.34]:
    38→
    39→Let $\alpha > 0$ be a real number, not an integer. The natural density of integers
    40→$n \geq 1$ for which $\gcd(n, \lfloor n^\alpha \rfloor) = 1$ equals $6/\pi^2$.
    41→
    42→The constant $6/\pi^2 = 1/\zeta(2)$ is the "probability" that two random integers
    43→are coprime, so this says $n$ and $\lfloor n^\alpha \rfloor$ behave like independent
    44→random integers with respect to coprimality when $\alpha$ is not an integer.
    45→
    46→This was proved by Bergelson and Richter [BeRi17].
    47→-/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    49→theorem erdos_1149 (α : ℝ) (hα_pos : 0 < α) (hα_not_int : ∀ k : ℤ, (k : ℝ) ≠ α) :
    50→    Tendsto (fun x : ℕ =>
    51→      (((Finset.Icc 1 x).filter (fun n =>
    52→        Nat.Coprime n (⌊(n : ℝ) ^ α⌋₊))).card : ℝ) / (x : ℝ))
    53→      atTop (nhds (6 / Real.pi ^ 2)) := by
    54→  sorry
    55→
    56→end Erdos1149
    57→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1151
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1151](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1151)
    23→
    24→Let $\mathcal{L}^n f(x) = \sum_{i} f(a_i) \ell_i(x)$ be the Lagrange interpolation
    25→polynomial of degree $n-1$ agreeing with $f$ at the $n$ Chebyshev nodes.
    26→
    27→Erdős [Er41] proved that for $x = \cos(\pi p/q)$ with $p, q$ odd, there is a continuous
    28→$f$ with $\lim_{n \to \infty} \mathcal{L}^n f(x) = \infty$. In [Er43] he claims
    29→(without proof) that for any closed set $A$ there exists continuous $f$ achieving $A$
    30→as the limit set.
    31→
    32→[Er41] Erdős, P., _On divergence properties of the Lagrange interpolation parabolas_.
    33→Annals of Mathematics, Series 2 (1941), 309–315.
    34→
    35→[Er43] Erdős, P., _A note on Farey series_. Quarterly Journal of Mathematics, Oxford
    36→Series (1943), 82–85.
    37→
    38→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    39→"Paul Erdős and his mathematics," Budapest, July 1999.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open Classical Finset BigOperators Filter
    43→
    44→open scoped Topology
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos1151
    47→
    48→/-- The $k$-th Chebyshev node of order $n$ (0-indexed):
    49→$\cos((2k + 1)\pi / (2n))$ for $k = 0, \ldots, n-1$. -/
    50→noncomputable def chebyshevNode (n : ℕ) (k : Fin n) : ℝ :=
    51→  Real.cos ((2 * (k : ℝ) + 1) * Real.pi / (2 * (n : ℝ)))
    52→
    53→/-- The Lagrange basis polynomial $\ell_i(x) = \prod_{j \neq i} (x - x_j)/(x_i - x_j)$. -/
    54→noncomputable def lagrangeBasis {n : ℕ} (nodes : Fin n → ℝ) (i : Fin n) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    55→  ∏ j ∈ Finset.univ.erase i, (x - nodes j) / (nodes i - nodes j)
    56→
    57→/-- The Lagrange interpolation of $f$ at the given nodes, evaluated at $x$:
    58→$L(x) = \sum_i f(x_i) \cdot \ell_i(x)$. -/
    59→noncomputable def lagrangeInterp {n : ℕ} (nodes : Fin n → ℝ) (f : ℝ → ℝ) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    60→  ∑ i : Fin n, f (nodes i) * lagrangeBasis nodes i x
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős Problem 1151 [Va99, 2.41]:
    64→For any $x \in [-1,1]$ and any closed $A \subseteq [-1,1]$, there exists a continuous
    65→function $f$ such that $A$ is the set of cluster points of the Lagrange interpolation
    66→polynomials $L^n f(x)$ at the Chebyshev nodes as $n \to \infty$.
    67→-/
    68→@[category research open, AMS 26 41]
    69→theorem erdos_1151 (x : ℝ) (hx : x ∈ Set.Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1)
    70→    (A : Set ℝ) (hA : IsClosed A) (hAsub : A ⊆ Set.Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1) :
    71→    ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, Continuous f ∧
    72→      {y | MapClusterPt y atTop (fun n => lagrangeInterp (chebyshevNode (n + 1)) f x)} = A := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→/--
    76→Variant of Erdős Problem 1151 restricted to the special points from [Er41]:
    77→For $x = \cos(\pi p / q)$ with $p, q$ odd positive integers and any closed
    78→$A \subseteq [-1,1]$, there exists a continuous function $f$ such that $A$ is
    79→the set of cluster points of the Lagrange interpolation polynomials at the
    80→Chebyshev nodes. This is the more conservative reading of Erdős's claim in [Er43].
    81→-/
    82→@[category research open, AMS 26 41]
    83→theorem erdos_1151_variant (p q : ℕ) (hp : Odd p) (hq : Odd q) (hq_pos : 0 < q)
    84→    (A : Set ℝ) (hA : IsClosed A) (hAsub : A ⊆ Set.Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1) :
    85→    let x := Real.cos (↑p * Real.pi / ↑q)
    86→    ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, Continuous f ∧
    87→      {y | MapClusterPt y atTop (fun n => lagrangeInterp (chebyshevNode (n + 1)) f x)} = A := by
    88→  sorry
    89→
    90→end Erdos1151
    91→

    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1152
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1152](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1152)
    23→
    24→For $n \geq 1$ fix some sequence of $n$ distinct numbers
    25→$x_{1,n}, \ldots, x_{n,n} \in [-1,1]$. Let $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(n) \to 0$.
    26→
    27→Does there always exist a continuous function $f : [-1,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ such that if
    28→$p_n$ is a sequence of polynomials, with degrees $\deg p_n < (1 + \varepsilon(n))n$, such
    29→that $p_n(x_{k,n}) = f(x_{k,n})$ for all $1 \leq k \leq n$, then $p_n(x) \not\to f(x)$
    30→for almost all $x \in [-1,1]$?
    31→
    32→Erdős, Kroó, and Szabados [EKS89] proved that if $\varepsilon > 0$ is fixed (does not
    33→$\to 0$), then there exist sequences $x_{i,j}$ such that for any continuous function $f$,
    34→there exists a sequence of polynomials $p_n$ with $\deg p_n < (1+\varepsilon)n$ interpolating
    35→$f$ at $x_{k,n}$, and $p_n(x) \to f(x)$ uniformly for all $x \in [-1,1]$.
    36→
    37→[EKS89] Erdős, P., Kroó, A., and Szabados, J., _On convergent interpolatory polynomials_
    38→(1989).
    39→
    40→[Va99] Vértesi, P., _Classical (unweighted) and weighted interpolation_ (1999).
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open Filter Polynomial MeasureTheory Set
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos1152
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Erdős Problem 1152 [Va99, 2.42]:
    49→
    50→For any triangular array of distinct interpolation nodes in $[-1,1]$ and any function
    51→$\varepsilon(n) \to 0$, there exists a continuous function $f : [-1,1] \to \mathbb{R}$
    52→such that every sequence of polynomials $p_n$ with $\deg p_n < (1 + \varepsilon(n))n$
    53→interpolating $f$ at the nodes fails to converge to $f$ for almost every $x \in [-1,1]$.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research open, AMS 41]
    56→theorem erdos_1152 : answer(sorry) ↔
    57→    ∀ (x : (n : ℕ) → Fin n → ℝ),
    58→      (∀ n, ∀ k : Fin n, x n k ∈ Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1) →
    59→      (∀ n, Function.Injective (x n)) →
    60→      ∀ (ε : ℕ → ℝ),
    61→        (∀ n, 0 < ε n) →
    62→        Tendsto ε atTop (nhds 0) →
    63→        ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, ContinuousOn f (Icc (-1) 1) ∧
    64→          ∀ p : ℕ → Polynomial ℝ,
    65→            (∀ n, n ≥ 1 → ((p n).natDegree : ℝ) < (1 + ε n) * n) →
    66→            (∀ n, ∀ k : Fin n, (p n).eval (x n k) = f (x n k)) →
    67→            ∀ᵐ t ∂(volume.restrict (Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1)),
    68→              ¬Tendsto (fun n => (p n).eval t) atTop (nhds (f t)) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos1152
    72→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1153
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1153](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1153)
    23→
    24→For $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in [-1,1]$, define the Lagrange basis polynomials
    25→$$\ell_k(x) = \prod_{i \neq k} \frac{x - x_i}{x_k - x_i},$$
    26→so that $\ell_k(x_k) = 1$ and $\ell_k(x_i) = 0$ for $i \neq k$.
    27→
    28→Let $\lambda(x) = \sum_k |\ell_k(x)|$ (the Lebesgue function).
    29→
    30→Is it true that, for any fixed $-1 \leq a < b \leq 1$,
    31→$$\max_{x \in [a,b]} \lambda(x) > \left(\frac{2}{\pi} - o(1)\right) \log n?$$
    32→
    33→Bernstein [Be31] proved this for $a = -1$ and $b = 1$, and Erdős [Er61c] improved this to
    34→$\max_{x \in [-1,1]} \lambda(x) > \frac{2}{\pi} \log n - O(1)$.
    35→This is best possible, since taking the $x_i$ as the roots of the $n$th Chebyshev
    36→polynomial yields $\max_{x \in [-1,1]} \lambda(x) < \frac{2}{\pi} \log n + O(1)$.
    37→
    38→The conjecture asks whether the same lower bound (up to $o(1)$ in the coefficient)
    39→holds when the maximum is restricted to any subinterval $[a,b] \subseteq [-1,1]$.
    40→
    41→See also related problems [#1129](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1129) and
    42→[#1132](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1132).
    43→
    44→[Er61c] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on the theory of interpolation. II_,
    45→  Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. (1961), 235–244.
    46→
    47→[Be31] Bernstein, S., _Sur la limitation des valeurs d'un polynome $P_n(x)$ de degré $n$ sur
    48→  tout un segment par ses valeurs en $(n+1)$ points du segment_, Izv. Akad. Nauk. SSSR (1931),
    49→  1025–1050.
    50→
    51→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    52→  "Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest, July 1999 (1999), §2.44.
    53→-/
    54→
    55→open Finset BigOperators Set
    56→
    57→namespace Erdos1153
    58→
    59→/-- The Lagrange basis polynomial $\ell_k(x)$ for nodes indexed by $\mathrm{Fin}\, n$.
    60→$\ell_k(x) = \prod_{i \neq k} (x - x_i) / (x_k - x_i)$. -/
    61→noncomputable def lagrangeBasis {n : ℕ} (nodes : Fin n → ℝ) (k : Fin n) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    62→  ∏ i ∈ univ.erase k, (x - nodes i) / (nodes k - nodes i)
    63→
    64→/-- The Lebesgue function: $\lambda(x) = \sum_k |\ell_k(x)|$. -/
    65→noncomputable def lebesgueFunction {n : ℕ} (nodes : Fin n → ℝ) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    66→  ∑ k, |lagrangeBasis nodes k x|
    67→
    68→/--
    69→Erdős Problem 1153:
    70→
    71→For any fixed $-1 \leq a < b \leq 1$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that
    72→for all $n \geq N$, for any choice of $n$ distinct nodes $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in [-1,1]$,
    73→$$\max_{x \in [a,b]} \sum_k |\ell_k(x)| > \left(\frac{2}{\pi} - \varepsilon\right) \log n.$$
    74→-/
    75→@[category research solved, AMS 41]
    76→theorem erdos_1153 : answer(True) ↔
    77→    ∀ a b : ℝ, a < b → -1 ≤ a → b ≤ 1 →
    78→      ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    79→        ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    80→          ∀ nodes : Fin n → ℝ,
    81→            Function.Injective nodes →
    82→            (∀ i, nodes i ∈ Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1) →
    83→            ∃ x ∈ Icc a b,
    84→              lebesgueFunction nodes x > (2 / Real.pi - ε) * Real.log (n : ℝ) := by
    85→  sorry
    86→
    87→end Erdos1153
    88→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1154
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1154](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1154)
    23→
    24→Does there exist, for every α ∈ [0,1], a subring of ℝ with Hausdorff dimension α?
    25→
    26→[Er79h] Erdős, P., _Some unconventional problems in number theory_. Math. Mag. 52 (1979), p. 119.
    27→
    28→[Va99] Varga, R.S., _Scientific Computation on Mathematical Problems and Conjectures_, 1999.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open MeasureTheory
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos1154
    34→
    35→/--
    36→Erdős Problem #1154 [Er79h, p.119]:
    37→
    38→Does there exist, for every $\alpha \in [0,1]$, a subring of $\mathbb{R}$ with Hausdorff
    39→dimension $\alpha$?
    40→
    41→Erdős and Volkmann proved the analogous result for subgroups of $\mathbb{R}$.
    42→Falconer showed that any subring with Hausdorff dimension $\alpha \in (1/2,1)$ cannot be
    43→Borel or Suslin. Edgar and Miller proved that any Borel or analytic subring of $\mathbb{R}$
    44→either has Hausdorff dimension $0$ or equals $\mathbb{R}$. Mauldin proved the result for
    45→subfields assuming the continuum hypothesis.
    46→-/
    47→@[category research open, AMS 13 28]
    48→theorem erdos_1154 : answer(sorry) ↔
    49→    ∀ (α : ℝ), 0 ≤ α → α ≤ 1 →
    50→      ∃ S : Subring ℝ, dimH (↑S : Set ℝ) = ENNReal.ofReal α := by
    51→  sorry
    52→
    53→end Erdos1154
    54→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1155
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1155](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1155)
    23→
    24→Construct a random graph on $n$ vertices in the following way: begin with the complete graph
    25→$K_n$. At each stage, choose uniformly a random triangle in the graph and delete all the edges
    26→of this triangle. Repeat until the graph is triangle-free. If $f(n)$ is the number of edges
    27→remaining, is it true that $\mathbb{E}[f(n)] \asymp n^{3/2}$ and that $f(n) \ll n^{3/2}$
    28→almost surely?
    29→
    30→A problem of Bollobás and Erdős [Bo98, p.231][Va99, 3.61].
    31→
    32→Bohman, Frieze, and Lubetzky [BFL15] proved that $f(n) = n^{3/2 + o(1)}$ a.s.
    33→
    34→[Bo98] Bollobás, B., _Modern Graph Theory_, Graduate Texts in Mathematics 184, Springer (1998).
    35→
    36→[Va99] Vu, V. H. (1999), 3.61.
    37→
    38→[Gr97] Grable, D. A., _On random greedy triangle packing_, Electronic Journal of
    39→Combinatorics 4 (1997), Research Paper 11.
    40→
    41→[BFL15] Bohman, T., Frieze, A., and Lubetzky, E., _Random triangle removal_,
    42→Advances in Mathematics 280 (2015), 379--438.
    43→-/
    44→
    45→open SimpleGraph Filter
    46→
    47→open scoped Topology
    48→
    49→namespace Erdos1155
    50→
    51→/-- The triangle removal process on $K_n$: starting from the complete graph on $n$
    52→    vertices, repeatedly choose a uniformly random triangle and remove all three
    53→    of its edges, until the graph is triangle-free.
    54→
    55→    `triangleRemovalExpectedEdges n` is $\mathbb{E}[f(n)]$, the expected number of edges
    56→    remaining when the process terminates. -/
    57→opaque triangleRemovalExpectedEdges (n : ℕ) : ℝ
    58→
    59→/-- The probability that the number of edges remaining after the triangle
    60→    removal process on $K_n$ satisfies a given predicate $P$. -/
    61→opaque triangleRemovalEdgeProb (n : ℕ) (P : ℕ → Prop) : ℝ
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Erdős Problem #1155, Part 1 [Bo98, p.231]:
    65→
    66→$\mathbb{E}[f(n)] \asymp n^{3/2}$, i.e., there exist constants $c_1, c_2 > 0$ such that for all
    67→sufficiently large $n$, $c_1 \cdot n^{3/2} \leq \mathbb{E}[f(n)] \leq c_2 \cdot n^{3/2}$.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research open, AMS 5 60]
    70→theorem erdos_1155 : answer(sorry) ↔
    71→    ∃ c₁ c₂ : ℝ, 0 < c₁ ∧ 0 < c₂ ∧
    72→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    73→        c₁ * (n : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2) ≤ triangleRemovalExpectedEdges n ∧
    74→        triangleRemovalExpectedEdges n ≤ c₂ * (n : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2) := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→/--
    78→Erdős Problem #1155, Part 2 [Bo98, p.231]:
    79→
    80→$f(n) \ll n^{3/2}$ almost surely, i.e., there exists $C > 0$ such that with
    81→probability tending to $1$, $f(n) \leq C \cdot n^{3/2}$.
    82→-/
    83→@[category research open, AMS 5 60]
    84→theorem erdos_1155.variants.almost_sure : answer(sorry) ↔
    85→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
    86→      Tendsto (fun n : ℕ =>
    87→        triangleRemovalEdgeProb n (fun k => (k : ℝ) ≤ C * (n : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2)))
    88→        atTop (nhds 1) := by
    89→  sorry
    90→
    91→/--
    92→Erdős Problem #1155, Variant (Bohman–Frieze–Lubetzky [BFL15]):
    93→
    94→$f(n) = n^{3/2 + o(1)}$ almost surely, i.e., for every $\varepsilon > 0$, the probability that
    95→$n^{3/2 - \varepsilon} \leq f(n) \leq n^{3/2 + \varepsilon}$ tends to $1$ as $n \to \infty$.
    96→This strengthens Part 2 by providing a matching almost-sure lower bound.
    97→-/
    98→@[category research solved, AMS 5 60]
    99→theorem erdos_1155.variants.bfl15 : answer(sorry) ↔
   100→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
   101→      Tendsto (fun n : ℕ =>
   102→        triangleRemovalEdgeProb n (fun k =>
   103→          (n : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2 - ε) ≤ (k : ℝ) ∧
   104→          (k : ℝ) ≤ (n : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) / 2 + ε)))
   105→        atTop (nhds 1) := by
   106→  sorry
   107→
   108→end Erdos1155
   109→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1156
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1156](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1156)
    23→
    24→Let $G$ be a random graph on $n$ vertices, in which every edge is included
    25→independently with probability $1/2$ (the Erdős–Rényi model $G(n, 1/2)$).
    26→
    27→Bollobás [Bo88] proved that $\chi(G) \sim n / (2 \log_2 n)$ with high probability.
    28→Shamir and Spencer [ShSp87] proved concentration within $o(\sqrt{n})$.
    29→Heckel and Riordan [HeRi23] proved concentration cannot be within $n^c$ for $c < 1/2$.
    30→
    31→[AlSp92] Alon, N. and Spencer, J., _The Probabilistic Method_, Wiley, 1992.
    32→
    33→[AlSp16] Alon, N. and Spencer, J. H., _The Probabilistic Method_, 4th ed., Wiley, 2016.
    34→
    35→[Bo88] Bollobás, B., _The chromatic number of random graphs_. Combinatorica (1988), 49–55.
    36→
    37→[He21] Heckel, A., _Non-concentration of the chromatic number of a random graph_.
    38→J. Amer. Math. Soc. (2021), 245–260.
    39→
    40→[HeRi23] Heckel, A. and Riordan, O., _How does the chromatic number of a random graph vary?_
    41→J. Lond. Math. Soc. (2) (2023), 1769–1815.
    42→
    43→[Sc17] Scott, A., _On the concentration of the chromatic number of random graphs_.
    44→arXiv:0806.0178 (2017).
    45→
    46→[ShSp87] Shamir, E. and Spencer, J., _Sharp concentration of the chromatic number on random
    47→graphs $G_{n,p}$_. Combinatorica (1987), 121–129.
    48→
    49→[Va99] Vu, V. H. (1999), 3.64.
    50→-/
    51→
    52→open Filter
    53→
    54→open scoped Topology
    55→
    56→namespace Erdos1156
    57→
    58→/-- The probability that the chromatic number of a uniformly random graph
    59→$G(n, 1/2)$ on $n$ vertices satisfies predicate $P$. Here $G(n, 1/2)$ is the
    60→Erdős–Rényi model where each edge is included independently with
    61→probability $1/2$, equivalently the uniform distribution over all simple
    62→graphs on $n$ labelled vertices. -/
    63→opaque chromaticNumberProb (n : ℕ) (P : ℕ → Prop) : ℝ
    64→
    65→/--
    66→Erdős Problem 1156, Part 1 [AlSp92]:
    67→
    68→There exists a constant $C$ such that $\chi(G(n, 1/2))$ is almost surely concentrated
    69→on at most $C$ values. That is, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ and all sufficiently large $n$,
    70→there is a set $S$ of at most $C$ natural numbers with
    71→$\mathbb{P}(\chi(G) \in S) \geq 1 - \varepsilon$.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research open, AMS 5 60]
    74→theorem erdos_1156 :
    75→    ∃ C : ℕ, ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    76→      ∀ᶠ n in atTop,
    77→        ∃ S : Finset ℕ, S.card ≤ C ∧
    78→          chromaticNumberProb n (· ∈ S) ≥ 1 - ε := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→/--
    82→Erdős Problem 1156, Part 2 [AlSp92] [Va99]:
    83→
    84→There exists a function $\omega : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $\omega(n) \to \infty$ such that
    85→for every function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    86→$$
    87→\mathbb{P}(|\chi(G) - f(n)| < \omega(n)) < 1/2.
    88→$$
    89→That is, the chromatic number cannot be concentrated in any interval of width
    90→$2\omega(n)$ with probability $\geq 1/2$.
    91→-/
    92→@[category research open, AMS 5 60]
    93→theorem erdos_1156.variants.anticoncentration :
    94→    ∃ ω : ℕ → ℝ, Tendsto ω atTop atTop ∧
    95→      ∀ f : ℕ → ℝ, ∀ᶠ n in atTop,
    96→        chromaticNumberProb n (fun k => |(k : ℝ) - f n| < ω n) < 1 / 2 := by
    97→  sorry
    98→
    99→end Erdos1156
   100→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1157
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1157](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1157)
    23→
    24→Let $r \geq 2$. Let $F$ be the family of all $r$-uniform hypergraphs with $k$ vertices
    25→and $s$ edges. Determine $\mathrm{ex}_r(n, F)$.
    26→
    27→Here $\mathrm{ex}_r(n, F) = f_r(n; k, s)$, the maximum number of edges in an $r$-uniform
    28→hypergraph on $n$ vertices such that no $k$ vertices span $s$ or more edges.
    29→
    30→Known lower bound (Brown, Erdős, Sós [BES73]): for all $k > r$ and $s > 1$,
    31→$$f_r(n; k, s) \gg n^{(rs-k)/(s-1)}.$$
    32→
    33→The general conjecture of Brown, Erdős, and Sós is that, for all $r > t \geq 2$
    34→and $s \geq 3$,
    35→$$f_r(n; k, s) = o(n^t)$$
    36→whenever $k \geq (r - t)s + t + 1$.
    37→
    38→The case $t = 2$ is problem [1178]. The case $r = 3$, $k = 6$, $s = 3$ is problem [716]
    39→(proved by Ruzsa–Szemerédi). The case $r = 3$ and $k = s + 2$ is problem [1076].
    40→
    41→[BES73] Brown, W.G., Erdős, P., and Sós, V.T., _Some extremal problems on r-graphs_.
    42→New Directions in the Theory of Graphs (1973), 53–63.
    43→
    44→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    45→"Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest, July 1999 (1999), §3.64.
    46→-/
    47→
    48→open Finset
    49→
    50→namespace Erdos1157
    51→
    52→/-- An $r$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices: a family of $r$-element subsets of
    53→$\operatorname{Fin}(n)$. -/
    54→structure UniformHypergraph (r n : ℕ) where
    55→  edges : Finset (Finset (Fin n))
    56→  uniform : ∀ e ∈ edges, e.card = r
    57→
    58→/-- The number of edges of $H$ spanned by a set $S$ of vertices. -/
    59→def edgesSpannedBy {r n : ℕ} (H : UniformHypergraph r n) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : ℕ :=
    60→  (H.edges.filter (· ⊆ S)).card
    61→
    62→/-- $H$ is $(k,s)$-free if no set of $k$ vertices spans $s$ or more edges. -/
    63→def IsFree {r n : ℕ} (H : UniformHypergraph r n) (k s : ℕ) : Prop :=
    64→  ∀ S : Finset (Fin n), S.card = k → edgesSpannedBy H S < s
    65→
    66→/-- $f_r(n; k, s)$: the maximum number of edges in an $r$-uniform $(k,s)$-free
    67→hypergraph on $n$ vertices. -/
    68→noncomputable def extremalNumber (r n k s : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    69→  sSup {m : ℕ | ∃ H : UniformHypergraph r n, IsFree H k s ∧ H.edges.card = m}
    70→
    71→/--
    72→Erdős Problem #1157 — Brown–Erdős–Sós Conjecture (general form) [BES73, Va99]:
    73→
    74→For all $r > t \geq 2$, $s \geq 3$, and $k \geq (r - t) \cdot s + t + 1$, we have
    75→$$f_r(n; k, s) = o(n^t),$$
    76→i.e., for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    77→$$f_r(n; k, s) \leq \varepsilon \cdot n^t.$$
    78→-/
    79→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    80→theorem erdos_1157 (r t : ℕ) (hr : r > t) (ht : t ≥ 2)
    81→    (s : ℕ) (hs : s ≥ 3) (k : ℕ) (hk : k ≥ (r - t) * s + t + 1) :
    82→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    83→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    84→      (extremalNumber r n k s : ℝ) ≤ ε * (n : ℝ) ^ t := by
    85→  sorry
    86→
    87→end Erdos1157
    88→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1158
    21→
    22→Erdős asks whether the extremal number for complete $t$-partite $t$-uniform hypergraphs satisfies
    23→$\mathrm{ex}_t(n, K_t(r)) \geq n^{t - r^{1-t} - o(1)}$.
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1158](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1158)
    26→
    27→[Er64f] Erdős, P., _On extremal problems of graphs and generalized graphs_. Israel J. Math. 2
    28→(1964), 183-190.
    29→
    30→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    31→"Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest, July 1999 (1999), §3.65.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1158
    35→
    36→/-- A $t$-uniform hypergraph on $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$: every edge has exactly $t$ vertices. -/
    37→def IsUniformHypergraph (t : ℕ) {n : ℕ} (E : Finset (Finset (Fin n))) : Prop :=
    38→  ∀ e ∈ E, e.card = t
    39→
    40→/-- A $t$-uniform hypergraph $E$ on $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$ contains a copy of the complete
    41→$t$-partite $t$-uniform hypergraph $K_t(r)$ if there exist $t$ pairwise disjoint vertex classes,
    42→each of size $r$, such that every transversal forms an edge of $E$. -/
    43→def HasKtrCopy (t r : ℕ) {n : ℕ} (E : Finset (Finset (Fin n))) : Prop :=
    44→  ∃ classes : Fin t → Finset (Fin n),
    45→    (∀ i, (classes i).card = r) ∧
    46→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j → Disjoint (classes i) (classes j)) ∧
    47→    (∀ f : Fin t → Fin n, (∀ i, f i ∈ classes i) →
    48→      Finset.image f Finset.univ ∈ E)
    49→
    50→/--
    51→**Erdős Problem 1158** [Va99, 3.65]:
    52→
    53→Let $K_t(r)$ be the complete $t$-partite $t$-uniform hypergraph with $r$ vertices in
    54→each class. Is it true that $\mathrm{ex}_t(n, K_t(r)) \geq n^{t - r^{1-t} - o(1)}$ for all $t, r$?
    55→
    56→Erdős [Er64f] proved that
    57→$n^{t - O(r^{1-t})} \leq \mathrm{ex}_t(n, K_t(r)) \ll n^{t - r^{1-t}}$.
    58→This is only known when $t = 2$ and $2 \leq r \leq 3$. The case $t = 2$ is problem \#714.
    59→
    60→Formally: for all $t \geq 2$, $r \geq 2$, and $\varepsilon > 0$, for sufficiently large $n$,
    61→there exists a $t$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices with no $K_t(r)$ copy and at least
    62→$n^{t - r^{1-t} - \varepsilon}$ edges.
    63→-/
    64→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_1158 : answer(sorry) ↔
    66→    ∀ (t r : ℕ), 2 ≤ t → 2 ≤ r →
    67→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    68→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
    69→      ∃ E : Finset (Finset (Fin n)),
    70→        IsUniformHypergraph t E ∧
    71→        ¬HasKtrCopy t r E ∧
    72→        (E.card : ℝ) ≥ (n : ℝ) ^ ((t : ℝ) - (r : ℝ) ^ (1 - (t : ℝ)) - ε) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos1158
    76→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1159
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1159](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1159)
    23→
    24→Determine whether there exists a constant $C > 1$ such that every finite projective plane
    25→has a blocking set where no line is hit more than $C$ times. A blocking set is a set of
    26→points that meets every line.
    27→
    28→Erdős [Er81] posed the stronger question for all pairwise balanced block designs, not just
    29→projective planes. See also Problem #664 for a related (stronger) variant.
    30→
    31→Erdős, Silverman, and Stein [ESS83] proved this is true with
    32→$|S \cap \ell| \ll \log n$ for all lines $\ell$ (where $n$ is the order of the projective
    33→plane).
    34→
    35→[Er81] Erdős, P., _On the combinatorial problems which I would most like to see solved_,
    36→Combinatorica 1 (1981), 25–42.
    37→
    38→[ESS83] Erdős, P., Silverman, R., Stein, A., _Intersection properties of families
    39→containing sets of nearly the same size_, Ars Combinatoria (1983), 247–259.
    40→
    41→[Va99] Vardy, A.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→open Configuration Classical Finset
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos1159
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem 1159 [Va99, 4.70]:
    50→
    51→Does there exist an absolute constant $C > 1$ such that every finite projective plane
    52→has a set of points $S$ meeting every line in at least $1$ and at most $C$ points?
    53→-/
    54→@[category research open, AMS 5 51]
    55→theorem erdos_1159 : answer(sorry) ↔
    56→    ∃ C : ℕ, 1 < C ∧
    57→      ∀ (P L : Type) [Membership P L] [Fintype P] [Fintype L]
    58→        [Configuration.ProjectivePlane P L],
    59→        ∃ S : Finset P,
    60→          ∀ l : L, 1 ≤ (S.filter (fun p => p ∈ l)).card ∧
    61→                    (S.filter (fun p => p ∈ l)).card ≤ C := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→/--
    65→Erdős–Silverman–Stein [ESS83] proved that every finite projective plane of order $n$ has a
    66→blocking set $S$ such that $|S \cap \ell| \ll \log n$ for all lines $\ell$.
    67→-/
    68→@[category research solved, AMS 5 51]
    69→theorem erdos_1159_ess_log_bound :
    70→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
    71→      ∀ (P L : Type) [Membership P L] [Fintype P] [Fintype L]
    72→        (pp : Configuration.ProjectivePlane P L),
    73→        ∃ S : Finset P,
    74→          ∀ l : L, 1 ≤ (S.filter (fun p => p ∈ l)).card ∧
    75→                    (S.filter (fun p => p ∈ l)).card ≤ ⌈C * Real.log pp.order⌉₊ := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos1159
    79→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1160
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1160](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1160)
    23→
    24→If $g(n)$ denotes the number of groups of order $n$, then $g(n) \le g(2^m)$ for all $n \le 2^m$.
    25→
    26→[Va99] Vaughan, R.C., _Problems in combinatorial number theory_, 1999.
    27→
    28→[BNV07] Blackburn, S.R., Neumann, P.M. and Venkataraman, G., _Enumeration of finite groups_,
    29→Cambridge Tracts in Mathematics, 2007, xii+281 pp.
    30→
    31→[Pa03] Pantelidakis, I., _On the Number of Non-isomorphic Groups of the Same Order_,
    32→DPhil Thesis, University of Oxford, 2003.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Classical
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos1160
    38→
    39→/-- Two group structures on the same type are isomorphic if there exists
    40→a multiplicative equivalence between them. -/
    41→def GroupStructIso (n : ℕ) (G₁ G₂ : Group (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    42→  Nonempty (@MulEquiv (Fin n) (Fin n) G₁.toMul G₂.toMul)
    43→
    44→/-- Group isomorphism is an equivalence relation on group structures on $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$. -/
    45→instance groupStructSetoid (n : ℕ) : Setoid (Group (Fin n)) where
    46→  r := GroupStructIso n
    47→  iseqv := by
    48→    refine ⟨?_, ?_, ?_⟩
    49→    · intro G
    50→      exact ⟨@MulEquiv.refl (Fin n) G.toMul⟩
    51→    · intro G₁ G₂ ⟨e⟩
    52→      exact ⟨@MulEquiv.symm (Fin n) (Fin n) G₁.toMul G₂.toMul e⟩
    53→    · intro G₁ G₂ G₃ ⟨e₁⟩ ⟨e₂⟩
    54→      exact ⟨@MulEquiv.trans (Fin n) (Fin n) (Fin n) G₁.toMul G₂.toMul G₃.toMul e₁ e₂⟩
    55→
    56→/-- The number of isomorphism classes of groups of order $n$ (OEIS A000001).
    57→Defined as the cardinality of group structures on $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$ modulo isomorphism. -/
    58→noncomputable def numGroupsOfOrder (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    59→  Nat.card (Quotient (groupStructSetoid n))
    60→
    61→/--
    62→Erdős Problem 1160 [Va99, 5.71]:
    63→
    64→Let $g(n)$ denote the number of groups of order $n$. If $n \le 2^m$ then $g(n) \le g(2^m)$.
    65→
    66→This conjecture states that among all $n \le 2^m$, the value $g(n)$ is maximized
    67→at $n = 2^m$ (i.e., powers of 2 have the most groups of any order up to that
    68→point). Listed as Question 22.16 in [BNV07], attributed to Erdős and Higman.
    69→-/
    70→@[category research open, AMS 20]
    71→theorem erdos_1160 (n m : ℕ) (h : n ≤ 2 ^ m) :
    72→    numGroupsOfOrder n ≤ numGroupsOfOrder (2 ^ m) := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→/--
    76→A stronger variant of Erdős Problem 1160, listed as Question 22.18 in [BNV07]:
    77→
    78→The sum $\sum_{n < 2^m} g(n) \le g(2^m)$ for all sufficiently large $m$ (perhaps $m \ge 7$).
    79→
    80→This asserts that $g(2^m)$ dominates not just each individual $g(n)$ for $n \le 2^m$,
    81→but the entire sum of all $g(n)$ for $n < 2^m$.
    82→-/
    83→@[category research open, AMS 20]
    84→theorem erdos_1160_sum_variant (m : ℕ) :
    85→    ∑ n ∈ Finset.range (2 ^ m), numGroupsOfOrder n ≤ numGroupsOfOrder (2 ^ m) := by
    86→  sorry
    87→
    88→end Erdos1160
    89→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1161
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [Va99, 5.72], [erdosproblems.com/1161](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1161)
    23→
    24→Let $f_k(n)$ count the number of elements of $S_n$ (the symmetric group) of
    25→order $k$. For which values of $k$ will $f_k(n)$ be maximal?
    26→
    27→Beker [Be25d] proved that
    28→$$\max_{k \geq 1} f_k(n) \sim (n-1)!,$$
    29→and characterized the maximizing values of $k$: for all large $n$,
    30→$f_k(n) = (n-1)!$ if and only if $k \geq 1$ is minimal such that
    31→$\operatorname{lcm}(1,\ldots,n-k) \mid k$.
    32→
    33→[Va99] Vardi, I., *Computational Recreations in Mathematica* (1999).
    34→[Be25d] Beker, A., _The most probable order of a random permutation_. arXiv:2510.11698 (2025).
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open Finset Equiv
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos1161
    40→
    41→/-- $f_k(n)$: the number of permutations in $S_n$ whose order equals $k$. -/
    42→noncomputable def countPermsOfOrder (n k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    43→  ((Finset.univ : Finset (Equiv.Perm (Fin n))).filter (fun σ => orderOf σ = k)).card
    44→
    45→/--
    46→Erdős Problem 1161 — asymptotic magnitude (Solved by Beker [Be25d]):
    47→
    48→Let $f_k(n)$ count the number of elements of $S_n$ of order $k$. Beker proved that
    49→$\max_{k \geq 1} f_k(n) \sim (n-1)!$, i.e., the maximum over $k$ of the number of
    50→permutations of order $k$ is asymptotic to $(n-1)!$.
    51→
    52→This formalizes only the asymptotic magnitude result. The characterization of which
    53→values of $k$ achieve the maximum is formalized separately in `erdos_1161_characterization`.
    54→
    55→Formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $n$,
    56→(1) there exists $k \geq 1$ with $f_k(n) \geq (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot (n-1)!$, and
    57→(2) for all $k$, $f_k(n) \leq (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot (n-1)!$.
    58→-/
    59→@[category research solved, AMS 5 20]
    60→theorem erdos_1161 :
    61→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    62→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    63→        (∃ k : ℕ, k ≥ 1 ∧
    64→          (countPermsOfOrder n k : ℝ) ≥ (1 - ε) * ((n - 1).factorial : ℝ)) ∧
    65→        (∀ k : ℕ,
    66→          (countPermsOfOrder n k : ℝ) ≤ (1 + ε) * ((n - 1).factorial : ℝ)) := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→/--
    70→Erdős Problem 1161 — characterization of maximizing $k$ (Solved by Beker [Be25d]):
    71→
    72→Beker proved that for all sufficiently large $n$, $f_k(n) = (n-1)!$ if and only if
    73→$k \geq 1$ is the minimal value such that $\operatorname{lcm}(1, \ldots, n-k) \mid k$.
    74→
    75→This complements `erdos_1161` which establishes the asymptotic magnitude
    76→$\max_{k \geq 1} f_k(n) \sim (n-1)!$.
    77→-/
    78→@[category research solved, AMS 5 20]
    79→theorem erdos_1161_characterization :
    80→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ → ∀ k : ℕ,
    81→      (countPermsOfOrder n k = (n - 1).factorial) ↔
    82→        (k ≥ 1 ∧
    83→         (Finset.lcm (Finset.range (n - k)) (· + 1)) ∣ k ∧
    84→         ∀ j : ℕ, j ≥ 1 → j < k →
    85→           ¬((Finset.lcm (Finset.range (n - j)) (· + 1)) ∣ j)) := by
    86→  sorry
    87→
    88→end Erdos1161
    89→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1162
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1162](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1162)
    23→
    24→Give an asymptotic formula for the number of subgroups of $S_n$.
    25→Is there a statistical theorem on their order?
    26→
    27→A problem of Erdős and Turán [Va99, 5.73].
    28→
    29→Let $f(n)$ count the number of subgroups of $S_n$.
    30→Pyber [Py93] proved that $\log f(n) \asymp n^2$.
    31→Roney-Dougal and Tracey [RoTr25] proved that $\log f(n) = (\tfrac{1}{16} + o(1))n^2$.
    32→
    33→See also Erdős Problem 1163 for a formalization of the "statistical theorem on subgroup orders" aspect.
    34→
    35→[Va99] Vardi, I., *Computational Recreations in Mathematica* (1999).
    36→[Py93] Pyber, L., _Asymptotic results for permutation groups_ (1993), 197–219.
    37→[RoTr25] Roney-Dougal, C., Tracey, G., _Subgroups of symmetric groups: enumeration and asymptotic properties_. arXiv:2503.05416 (2025).
    38→-/
    39→
    40→open Filter Real
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos1162
    43→
    44→/-- The number of subgroups of the symmetric group $S_n$. -/
    45→noncomputable def numSubgroups (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    46→  Nat.card (Subgroup (Equiv.Perm (Fin n)))
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem 1162 (partially resolved by Roney-Dougal and Tracey [RoTr25]):
    50→
    51→$\log_2 f(n) / n^2 \to \tfrac{1}{16}$ as $n \to \infty$, where $f(n)$ is the number of
    52→subgroups of $S_n$. Equivalently, $\ln f(n) / n^2 \to (\ln 2)/16$.
    53→
    54→The full problem, asking for an asymptotic formula for $f(n)$ and a statistical
    55→theorem on the orders of subgroups, remains open.
    56→-/
    57→@[category research solved, AMS 20]
    58→theorem erdos_1162 :
    59→    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => Real.log (numSubgroups n : ℝ) / ((n : ℝ) ^ 2))
    60→      atTop (nhds (Real.log 2 / 16)) := by
    61→  sorry
    62→
    63→end Erdos1162
    64→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1163
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1163](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1163)
    23→
    24→Describe (by statistical means) the arithmetic structure of the orders of
    25→subgroups of $S_n$. A concrete interpretation: the proportion of divisors of
    26→$n!$ that are orders of subgroups of $S_n$ tends to $0$ as $n \to \infty$.
    27→
    28→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the
    29→conference "Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest (1999).
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Equiv Classical
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1163
    35→
    36→/-- A natural number $m$ is a subgroup order of $S_n$ if there exists a subgroup
    37→of the symmetric group `Perm (Fin n)` with exactly $m$ elements. -/
    38→def IsSubgroupOrderOfSn (n m : ℕ) : Prop :=
    39→  ∃ H : Subgroup (Perm (Fin n)), Nat.card H = m
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 1163 (Erdős and Turán) [Va99, 5.74]:
    43→Describe (by statistical means) the arithmetic structure of the orders of
    44→subgroups of $S_n$.
    45→
    46→The original problem is acknowledged as ambiguous. A concrete
    47→interpretation: as $n \to \infty$, the proportion of divisors of $n!$ that are orders
    48→of subgroups of $S_n$ tends to $0$. By Lagrange's theorem, every subgroup order
    49→divides $n!$, but the set of subgroup orders becomes a vanishingly small
    50→fraction of all divisors.
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 5 20]
    53→theorem erdos_1163 :
    54→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    55→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n ≥ N,
    56→        ((n.factorial.divisors.filter (fun m => IsSubgroupOrderOfSn n m)).card : ℝ) <
    57→        ε * (n.factorial.divisors.card : ℝ) := by
    58→  sorry
    59→
    60→end Erdos1163
    61→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1164
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1164](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1164)
    23→
    24→Let $S_n$ denote a simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^2$ starting at the origin, and let $R_n$
    25→be the largest integer such that the walk visits every lattice point within distance $R_n$ of the
    26→origin in its first $n$ steps. Is it true that $\log R_n \asymp \sqrt{\log n}$ almost surely?
    27→
    28→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    29→"Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest, July 1999 (1999), §6.76.
    30→
    31→[Re90] Révész, P., _Random walk in random and nonrandom environments_, World Scientific, 1990.
    32→
    33→[DPRZ04] Dembo, A., Peres, Y., Rosen, J. and Zeitouni, O., _Cover times for Brownian motion
    34→and random walks in two dimensions_, Annals of Mathematics **160** (2004), 433–464.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open MeasureTheory ProbabilityTheory Filter Finset BigOperators
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos1164
    40→
    41→variable {Ω : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ω]
    42→
    43→/-- A step distribution for a simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^2$: the random variable takes
    44→values in $\{(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)\}$ each with equal probability. -/
    45→def IsUniformStep (μ : Measure Ω) (X : Ω → ℤ × ℤ) : Prop :=
    46→  (∀ ω, X ω ∈ ({((1 : ℤ), 0), (-1, 0), (0, 1), (0, -1)} : Set (ℤ × ℤ))) ∧
    47→  μ {ω | X ω = (1, 0)} = μ {ω | X ω = (-1, 0)} ∧
    48→  μ {ω | X ω = (-1, 0)} = μ {ω | X ω = (0, 1)} ∧
    49→  μ {ω | X ω = (0, 1)} = μ {ω | X ω = (0, -1)}
    50→
    51→/-- Position of the random walk at time $n$: $S_n = X_0 + X_1 + \cdots + X_{n-1}$,
    52→starting at the origin $S_0 = (0, 0)$. -/
    53→def walkPosition (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : ℤ × ℤ :=
    54→  ∑ i ∈ Finset.range n, X i ω
    55→
    56→/-- The covering radius $R_n(\omega)$: the largest $R \in \mathbb{N}$ such that every lattice
    57→point $(a, b) \in \mathbb{Z}^2$ with $a^2 + b^2 \le R^2$ is visited by the walk within its
    58→first $n$ steps. -/
    59→noncomputable def coveringRadius (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    60→  sSup {R : ℕ | ∀ (a b : ℤ), a ^ 2 + b ^ 2 ≤ ↑R ^ 2 →
    61→    ∃ k, k ≤ n ∧ walkPosition X ω k = (a, b)}
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Erdős Problem 1164 (Erdős–Taylor) [Va99, 6.76]:
    65→
    66→Let $R_n$ be the maximal integer such that almost every random walk from the origin
    67→in $\mathbb{Z}^2$ visits every $x \in \mathbb{Z}^2$ with $\|x\| \le R_n$ in at most $n$ steps.
    68→Is it true that $\log R_n \asymp \sqrt{\log n}$?
    69→
    70→That is, there exist constants $c_1, c_2 > 0$ such that almost surely, for all
    71→sufficiently large $n$:
    72→$$c_1 \cdot \sqrt{\log n} \le \log R_n \le c_2 \cdot \sqrt{\log n}.$$
    73→
    74→Proved independently by Révész [Re90] and Kesten. The stronger conjecture
    75→$$\lim P((\log R_n)^2 / \log n \le x) = e^{-4x} \quad \text{for all } x > 0$$
    76→was proved by Dembo, Peres, Rosen, and Zeitouni [DPRZ04].
    77→-/
    78→@[category research solved, AMS 60]
    79→theorem erdos_1164 : answer(True) ↔
    80→    ∀ (Ω : Type*) [MeasurableSpace Ω] (μ : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    81→      (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ),
    82→      (∀ i, IsUniformStep μ (X i)) → iIndepFun X μ →
    83→        ∃ c₁ c₂ : ℝ, 0 < c₁ ∧ 0 < c₂ ∧
    84→          ∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, ∀ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop,
    85→            c₁ * Real.sqrt (Real.log (n : ℝ)) ≤ Real.log (coveringRadius X ω n : ℝ) ∧
    86→            Real.log (coveringRadius X ω n : ℝ) ≤ c₂ * Real.sqrt (Real.log (n : ℝ)) := by
    87→  sorry
    88→
    89→/--
    90→Erdős Problem 1164 — Stronger distributional result [DPRZ04]:
    91→
    92→Dembo, Peres, Rosen, and Zeitouni proved the stronger conjecture that
    93→$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \mathbb{P}\left(\frac{(\log R_n)^2}{\log n} \le x\right) = e^{-4x}$$
    94→for all $x > 0$. This subsumes the original asymptotic statement `erdos_1164`.
    95→-/
    96→@[category research solved, AMS 60]
    97→theorem erdos_1164_strong :
    98→    ∀ (Ω : Type*) [MeasurableSpace Ω] (μ : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    99→      (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ),
   100→      (∀ i, IsUniformStep μ (X i)) → iIndepFun X μ →
   101→        ∀ x : ℝ, 0 < x →
   102→          Filter.Tendsto (fun n : ℕ =>
   103→            (μ {ω | (Real.log (coveringRadius X ω n : ℝ)) ^ 2 / Real.log (n : ℝ) ≤ x}).toReal)
   104→          Filter.atTop (nhds (Real.exp (-4 * x))) := by
   105→  sorry
   106→
   107→end Erdos1164
   108→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1165
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1165](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1165)
    23→
    24→Given a random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^2$, the *favourite sites* $F(n)$ are those visited the
    25→maximum number of times up to step $n$. Tóth proved that $|F(n)| = r$ occurs only finitely
    26→often almost surely for every $r \ge 4$, and Hao–Li–Okada–Zheng proved that $|F(n)| = 3$
    27→occurs infinitely often almost surely.
    28→
    29→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    30→"Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest, July 1999 (1999), §6.77.
    31→
    32→[To01] Tóth, B., _No more than three favorite sites for simple random walk_.
    33→Ann. Probab. (2001), 484–503.
    34→
    35→[HLOZ24] Hao, C., Li, X., Okada, I., Zheng, Y., _Favorite sites for simple random walk
    36→in two and more dimensions_. arXiv:2409.00995 (2024).
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open MeasureTheory ProbabilityTheory Filter Finset BigOperators
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos1165
    42→
    43→variable {Ω : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ω]
    44→
    45→/-- A step distribution for a simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^2$: the random variable takes
    46→values in $\{(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)\}$ each with equal probability. -/
    47→def IsUniformStep (μ : Measure Ω) (X : Ω → ℤ × ℤ) : Prop :=
    48→  (∀ ω, X ω ∈ ({((1 : ℤ), 0), (-1, 0), (0, 1), (0, -1)} : Set (ℤ × ℤ))) ∧
    49→  μ {ω | X ω = (1, 0)} = μ {ω | X ω = (-1, 0)} ∧
    50→  μ {ω | X ω = (-1, 0)} = μ {ω | X ω = (0, 1)} ∧
    51→  μ {ω | X ω = (0, 1)} = μ {ω | X ω = (0, -1)}
    52→
    53→/-- Position of the random walk at time $n$: $S_n = X_0 + X_1 + \cdots + X_{n-1}$,
    54→starting at the origin $S_0 = (0, 0)$. -/
    55→def walkPosition (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : ℤ × ℤ :=
    56→  ∑ i ∈ Finset.range n, X i ω
    57→
    58→/-- The local time (visit count) at site $x$ up to time $n$:
    59→$f_n(x) = |\{k : 0 \le k \le n \mid S_k = x\}|$. -/
    60→def localTime (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) (x : ℤ × ℤ) : ℕ :=
    61→  ((Finset.range (n + 1)).filter (fun k => walkPosition X ω k = x)).card
    62→
    63→/-- The set of sites visited by the walk up to time $n$. -/
    64→def visitedSites (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : Finset (ℤ × ℤ) :=
    65→  (Finset.range (n + 1)).image (fun k => walkPosition X ω k)
    66→
    67→/-- The maximum local time at time $n$:
    68→$\max_y f_n(y)$, the maximum number of visits to any single site. -/
    69→def maxLocalTime (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    70→  (visitedSites X ω n).sup (localTime X ω n)
    71→
    72→/-- The set of favourite sites at time $n$:
    73→$F(n) = \{x \in \text{visited sites} : f_n(x) = \max_y f_n(y)\}$. -/
    74→def favouriteSites (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : Finset (ℤ × ℤ) :=
    75→  (visitedSites X ω n).filter (fun x => localTime X ω n x = maxLocalTime X ω n)
    76→
    77→/--
    78→Erdős Problem 1165 (Erdős–Révész) [Va99, 6.77]:
    79→
    80→Given a random walk $s_0, s_1, \ldots$ in $\mathbb{Z}^2$, starting at the origin, let $f_n(x)$
    81→count the number of $0 \le k \le n$ such that $s_k = x$. Let
    82→$F(n) = \{x : f_n(x) = \max_y f_n(y)\}$ be the set of 'favourite sites'.
    83→
    84→Tóth [To01] proved that $|F(n)| = r$ occurs only finitely often almost surely for all $r \ge 4$.
    85→Hao, Li, Okada, and Zheng [HLOZ24] proved that $|F(n)| = 3$ occurs infinitely often almost surely.
    86→-/
    87→@[category research solved, AMS 60]
    88→theorem erdos_1165
    89→    {μ : Measure Ω} [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    90→    {X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ}
    91→    (hStep : ∀ i, IsUniformStep μ (X i))
    92→    (hIndep : iIndepFun X μ) :
    93→    -- Part 1: |F(n)| = 3 infinitely often, almost surely
    94→    (∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, ∃ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop,
    95→      (favouriteSites X ω n).card = 3) ∧
    96→    -- Part 2: |F(n)| = r for r ≥ 4 happens only finitely often, almost surely
    97→    (∀ r : ℕ, r ≥ 4 →
    98→      ∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, ¬∃ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop,
    99→        (favouriteSites X ω n).card = r) := by
   100→  sorry
   101→
   102→end Erdos1165
   103→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1166
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1166](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1166)
    23→
    24→For a simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^2$, a *favourite site* at time $k$ is a site with the
    25→maximum number of visits up to time $k$. The problem asks whether the cumulative number of
    26→distinct favourite sites $\left|\bigcup_{k \le n} F(k)\right|$ is bounded by a power of $\log n$
    27→almost surely, for all but finitely many $n$.
    28→
    29→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    30→"Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest, July 1999 (1999), §6.78.
    31→
    32→[ErTa60] Erdős, P. and Taylor, S. J., *Some problems concerning the structure of random walk
    33→paths*, Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. 11, 137–162, 1960.
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open MeasureTheory ProbabilityTheory Filter Finset
    37→
    38→open scoped BigOperators
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos1166
    41→
    42→variable {Ω : Type*} [MeasurableSpace Ω]
    43→
    44→/-- A step distribution for a simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}^2$: the random variable takes
    45→    values in $\{(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)\}$ each with equal probability. -/
    46→def IsUniformStep (μ : Measure Ω) (X : Ω → ℤ × ℤ) : Prop :=
    47→  (∀ ω, X ω ∈ ({((1 : ℤ), 0), (-1, 0), (0, 1), (0, -1)} : Set (ℤ × ℤ))) ∧
    48→  μ {ω | X ω = (1, 0)} = μ {ω | X ω = (-1, 0)} ∧
    49→  μ {ω | X ω = (-1, 0)} = μ {ω | X ω = (0, 1)} ∧
    50→  μ {ω | X ω = (0, 1)} = μ {ω | X ω = (0, -1)}
    51→
    52→/-- Position of the random walk at time $n$: $S_n = X_0 + X_1 + \cdots + X_{n-1}$,
    53→    starting at the origin $S_0 = (0, 0)$. -/
    54→def walkPosition (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : ℤ × ℤ :=
    55→  ∑ i ∈ Finset.range n, X i ω
    56→
    57→/-- The local time (visit count) at site $x$ up to time $k$:
    58→    $f_k(x) = |\{l : 0 \le l \le k \mid S_l = x\}|$. -/
    59→def localTime (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (k : ℕ) (x : ℤ × ℤ) : ℕ :=
    60→  ((Finset.range (k + 1)).filter (fun l => walkPosition X ω l = x)).card
    61→
    62→/-- The set of sites visited by the walk up to time $k$. -/
    63→def visitedSites (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (k : ℕ) : Finset (ℤ × ℤ) :=
    64→  (Finset.range (k + 1)).image (fun l => walkPosition X ω l)
    65→
    66→/-- The maximum local time at time $k$:
    67→    $\max_y f_k(y)$, the maximum number of visits to any single site. -/
    68→def maxLocalTime (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    69→  (visitedSites X ω k).sup (localTime X ω k)
    70→
    71→/-- The set of favourite sites at time $k$:
    72→    $F(k) = \{x \in \text{visited sites} : f_k(x) = \max_y f_k(y)\}$. -/
    73→def favouriteSites (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (k : ℕ) : Finset (ℤ × ℤ) :=
    74→  (visitedSites X ω k).filter (fun x => localTime X ω k x = maxLocalTime X ω k)
    75→
    76→/-- The cumulative set of favourite sites up to time $n$:
    77→    $\bigcup_{k \le n} F(k)$, the set of all sites that were ever a favourite site. -/
    78→def cumulativeFavouriteSites (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ) (ω : Ω) (n : ℕ) : Finset (ℤ × ℤ) :=
    79→  (Finset.range (n + 1)).biUnion (fun k => favouriteSites X ω k)
    80→
    81→/--
    82→Erdős Problem 1166 (Erdős–Révész) [Va99, 6.78]:
    83→
    84→Given a random walk $s_0, s_1, \ldots$ in $\mathbb{Z}^2$, starting at the origin, let $f_k(x)$
    85→count the number of $0 \le l \le k$ such that $s_l = x$. Let
    86→$F(k) = \{x : f_k(x) = \max_y f_k(y)\}$ be the set of 'favourite sites'. Is it true that
    87→$$\left|\bigcup_{k \le n} F(k)\right| \le (\log n)^{O(1)}$$
    88→almost surely, for all but finitely many $n$?
    89→
    90→This is true: almost surely $\left|\bigcup_{k \le n} F(k)\right| \ll (\log n)^2$, which follows
    91→from the fact that almost surely $|F(n)| \le 3$ for all large $n$ (see Erdős Problem 1165) and
    92→the result of Erdős and Taylor [ErTa60] that the maximum number of visits to any fixed point
    93→by time $n$ is $\ll (\log n)^2$.
    94→-/
    95→@[category research solved, AMS 60]
    96→theorem erdos_1166 : answer(True) ↔
    97→    ∀ (Ω : Type*) [MeasurableSpace Ω] (μ : Measure Ω) [IsProbabilityMeasure μ]
    98→      (X : ℕ → Ω → ℤ × ℤ),
    99→    (∀ i, IsUniformStep μ (X i)) →
   100→    iIndepFun X μ →
   101→    ∃ C : ℕ, ∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, ∀ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop,
   102→      ((cumulativeFavouriteSites X ω n).card : ℝ) ≤ Real.log (n : ℝ) ^ C := by
   103→  sorry
   104→
   105→end Erdos1166
   106→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1167
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1167](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1167)
    23→
    24→A problem of Erdős, Hajnal, and Rado on cardinal partition relations:
    25→does the stepping-up lemma hold for partition relations, i.e., does
    26→$2^\lambda \to (\kappa_\alpha + 1)^{r+1}$ imply $\lambda \to (\kappa_\alpha)^r$
    27→for $r \geq 2$ and $\lambda$ infinite?
    28→
    29→[Va99] Vaughan, J., *Small uncountable cardinals and topology*. Open Problems in Topology (1999).
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Cardinal
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1167
    35→
    36→/-- The cardinal partition relation $\kappa \to (\text{targets}(\alpha))_{\alpha : \iota}^r$:
    37→    for every coloring of the $r$-element subsets of a $\kappa$-sized set with colors from $\iota$,
    38→    there exists a color $i$ and a monochromatic subset of cardinality $\geq \text{targets}(i)$. -/
    39→def CardinalPartitionRel (κ : Cardinal) {ι : Type*} (targets : ι → Cardinal)
    40→    (r : ℕ) : Prop :=
    41→  ∀ (S : Type*) [DecidableEq S] (_ : #S = κ) (c : Finset S → ι),
    42→    ∃ (i : ι) (H : Set S),
    43→      Cardinal.mk H ≥ targets i ∧
    44→      ∀ s : Finset S, s.card = r → (↑s : Set S) ⊆ H → c s = i
    45→
    46→/-- **Erdős Problem 1167** [Va99, 7.79] (Erdős–Hajnal–Rado):
    47→    For $r \geq 2$ and $\lambda$ infinite, does
    48→    $2^\lambda \to (\kappa_\alpha + 1)^{r+1}$ imply $\lambda \to (\kappa_\alpha)^r$? -/
    49→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    50→theorem erdos_1167 : answer(sorry) ↔
    51→    ∀ {ι : Type*} (κ : ι → Cardinal) (lam : Cardinal) (r : ℕ),
    52→      r ≥ 2 → ℵ₀ ≤ lam →
    53→      CardinalPartitionRel ((2 : Cardinal) ^ lam) (fun α => κ α + 1) (r + 1) →
    54→      CardinalPartitionRel lam κ r := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos1167
    58→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1168
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1168](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1168)
    23→
    24→A problem of Erdős, Hajnal, and Rado on the negative partition relation
    25→$\aleph_{\omega+1} \nrightarrow (\aleph_{\omega+1}, 3, \ldots, 3)_{\aleph_0}^2$
    26→without assuming the generalised continuum hypothesis.
    27→
    28→[Va99] Vaughan, J., *Small uncountable cardinals and topology*. Open Problems in Topology (1999).
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open Cardinal Ordinal
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos1168
    34→
    35→/--
    36→Erdős Problem 1168 [Va99, 7.80]:
    37→
    38→The negative partition relation
    39→$\aleph_{\omega+1} \nrightarrow (\aleph_{\omega+1}, 3, \ldots, 3)_{\aleph_0}^2$:
    40→for any type $\alpha$ of cardinality $\aleph_{\omega+1}$, there exists a coloring of unordered
    41→pairs of elements of $\alpha$ with countably many colors (indexed by $\mathbb{N}$) such that:
    42→- No set of cardinality $\aleph_{\omega+1}$ is monochromatic in color $0$
    43→- No triple is monochromatic in any color $n \geq 1$
    44→-/
    45→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    46→theorem erdos_1168 :
    47→    ∀ (α : Type*) (_ : #α = ℵ_ (ω + 1)),
    48→    ∃ (c : α → α → ℕ),
    49→      -- The coloring is symmetric
    50→      (∀ a b, c a b = c b a) ∧
    51→      -- No monochromatic set of cardinality ℵ_{ω+1} for color 0
    52→      (∀ S : Set α, #S = ℵ_ (ω + 1) →
    53→        ∃ a ∈ S, ∃ b ∈ S, a ≠ b ∧ c a b ≠ 0) ∧
    54→      -- No monochromatic triple for any color n ≥ 1
    55→      (∀ (n : ℕ), n ≥ 1 →
    56→        ∀ a b d : α, a ≠ b → a ≠ d → b ≠ d →
    57→          ¬(c a b = n ∧ c a d = n ∧ c b d = n)) := by
    58→  sorry
    59→
    60→end Erdos1168
    61→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1169
    21→
    22→Erdős and Hajnal asked whether for all finite $k < \omega$, the ordinal partition
    23→relation $\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1^2, k)^2$ fails.
    24→
    25→Hajnal proved this holds assuming the Continuum Hypothesis. The problem is
    26→independent of ZFC: it is consistent with ZFC but not provable from ZFC alone.
    27→
    28→See also Problem 592 (a related question concerning countable ordinals)
    29→and Problem 1172 (which uses the same `OrdinalPartitionPair` definition).
    30→
    31→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1169](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1169)
    32→
    33→[Va99] Hajnal, A. and Larson, J., _Partition relations_. Handbook of Set Theory (2010), §7.85.
    34→
    35→[Ha71] Hajnal, A., _A negative partition relation_. Proceedings of the National Academy of
    36→  Sciences U.S.A. (1971), 142–144.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open Ordinal Cardinal
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos1169
    42→
    43→/-- $\omega_1$, the first uncountable ordinal. -/
    44→noncomputable def omega1 : Ordinal := (aleph 1).ord
    45→
    46→/-- The ordinal partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, \gamma)^2$ for 2-colorings of pairs.
    47→    For every 2-coloring of the pairs of ordinals below $\alpha$, there is either
    48→    a homogeneous set of order type $\beta$ in color 0, or a homogeneous set of
    49→    order type $\gamma$ in color 1. Formalized via strictly monotone embeddings:
    50→    a subset of order type $\beta$ corresponds to a strictly monotone function
    51→    from $\{x \mid x < \beta\}$ to $\{x \mid x < \alpha\}$. -/
    52→def OrdinalPartitionPair (α β γ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    53→  ∀ f : {x : Ordinal // x < α} → {x : Ordinal // x < α} → Bool,
    54→    (∃ g : {x : Ordinal // x < β} → {x : Ordinal // x < α},
    55→      StrictMono g ∧
    56→      ∀ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < β}, i < j → f (g i) (g j) = true) ∨
    57→    (∃ g : {x : Ordinal // x < γ} → {x : Ordinal // x < α},
    58→      StrictMono g ∧
    59→      ∀ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < γ}, i < j → f (g i) (g j) = false)
    60→
    61→/--
    62→Erdős Problem #1169 [Va99, 7.85] (Erdős and Hajnal):
    63→
    64→Is it true that, for all finite $k \geq 3$,
    65→$$\omega_1^2 \not\to (\omega_1^2, k)^2?$$
    66→
    67→That is, for every natural number $k \geq 3$, there exists a 2-coloring of the pairs
    68→of ordinals below $\omega_1^2$ such that no subset of order type $\omega_1^2$ is monochromatic
    69→in the first color and no subset of order type $k$ is monochromatic in the
    70→second color.
    71→
    72→Hajnal proved this holds assuming the Continuum Hypothesis [Ha71].
    73→The problem is independent of ZFC: consistent with ZFC but not provable
    74→from ZFC alone.
    75→
    76→The restriction to $k \geq 3$ is necessary because the partition relation
    77→$\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1^2, k)^2$ holds trivially for $k \leq 2$.
    78→-/
    79→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    80→theorem erdos_1169 : answer(sorry) ↔
    81→    ∀ k : ℕ, 3 ≤ k → ¬ OrdinalPartitionPair (omega1 ^ 2) (omega1 ^ 2) (↑k) := by
    82→  sorry
    83→
    84→end Erdos1169
    85→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1170
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1170](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1170)
    23→
    24→Erdős asked whether it is consistent that the ordinal partition relation
    25→$\omega_2 \to (\alpha)^2_2$ holds for every $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    26→
    27→- [Va99] Section 7.86.
    28→- [La82] Laver, R., _An (ℵ₂, ℵ₂, ℵ₀)-saturated ideal on ω₁_ (1982), 173–180.
    29→- [FoHa03] Foreman, M. and Hajnal, A., _A partition relation for successors of large cardinals_,
    30→  Math. Ann. (2003), 583–623.
    31→
    32→Known partial results:
    33→- [La82] Laver proved the consistency of $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot 2 + 1, \alpha)^2$
    34→  for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    35→- [FoHa03] Foreman–Hajnal proved the consistency of
    36→  $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1^2 + 1, \alpha)^2$ for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open Ordinal Cardinal
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos1170
    42→
    43→/-- $\omega_2$, the second uncountable ordinal (initial ordinal of cardinality $\aleph_2$). -/
    44→noncomputable def omega2 : Ordinal := (aleph 2).ord
    45→
    46→/-- The ordinal partition relation $\kappa \to (\alpha)^2_2$: for every 2-coloring of increasing
    47→pairs of ordinals below $\kappa$, there exists a monochromatic set of order type $\alpha$.
    48→Formalized via strictly monotone embeddings: a subset of order type $\alpha$ corresponds
    49→to a strictly monotone function from $\{x \mid x < \alpha\}$ to $\{x \mid x < \kappa\}$. -/
    50→def OrdinalPartition (κ α : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    51→  ∀ f : {x : Ordinal // x < κ} → {x : Ordinal // x < κ} → Bool,
    52→    ∃ (c : Bool) (g : {x : Ordinal // x < α} → {x : Ordinal // x < κ}),
    53→      StrictMono g ∧
    54→      ∀ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < α}, i < j → f (g i) (g j) = c
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Is it consistent that $\omega_2 \to (\alpha)^2_2$ for every $\alpha < \omega_2$?
    58→
    59→The arrow notation $\kappa \to (\alpha)^2_2$ denotes the ordinal partition relation: for every
    60→2-coloring of pairs of ordinals below $\kappa$, there exists a monochromatic set of
    61→order type $\alpha$.
    62→
    63→This is a consistency question: is there a model of ZFC in which this holds?
    64→We formalize the property itself.
    65→
    66→Known partial results:
    67→- Laver [La82] proved the consistency of $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot 2 + 1, \alpha)^2$
    68→  for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    69→- Foreman–Hajnal [FoHa03] proved the consistency of
    70→  $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1^2 + 1, \alpha)^2$ for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    73→theorem erdos_1170 : answer(sorry) ↔
    74→    ∀ α : Ordinal, α < omega2 →
    75→      OrdinalPartition omega2 α := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos1170
    79→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1171
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1171](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1171)
    23→
    24→Erdős asked whether for all finite $k$, the ordinal partition relation
    25→$\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, 3, \ldots, 3)^2_{k+1}$ holds — that is, every
    26→$(k+1)$-coloring of pairs below $\omega_1^2$ yields either a homogeneous set of order type
    27→$\omega_1 \cdot \omega$ for the first color or a monochromatic triple for some other color.
    28→
    29→- [Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the conference
    30→"Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest, July 1999 (1999), §7.84.
    31→- [Ba89b] Baumgartner, J. E., _Remarks on partition ordinals_ (1989), 5–17.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Ordinal Cardinal
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos1171
    37→
    38→/-- $\omega_1$, the first uncountable ordinal. -/
    39→noncomputable def omega1 : Ordinal := (aleph 1).ord
    40→
    41→/-- The multi-color ordinal partition relation
    42→$\alpha \to (\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_1)^2$ with $(k+1)$ colors,
    43→where $\beta_1$ appears $k$ times.
    44→For every $(k+1)$-coloring of pairs of ordinals below $\alpha$, either
    45→there is a homogeneous set of order type $\beta_0$ for color $0$, or there exists some
    46→color $c > 0$ with a homogeneous set of order type $\beta_1$. -/
    47→def OrdinalPartitionMulticolor (α : Ordinal) (k : ℕ) (target₀ target_rest : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    48→  ∀ f : {x : Ordinal // x < α} → {x : Ordinal // x < α} → Fin (k + 1),
    49→    (∃ g : {x : Ordinal // x < target₀} → {x : Ordinal // x < α},
    50→      StrictMono g ∧
    51→      ∀ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < target₀}, i < j → f (g i) (g j) = 0) ∨
    52→    (∃ c : Fin (k + 1), 0 < c.val ∧
    53→      ∃ g : {x : Ordinal // x < target_rest} → {x : Ordinal // x < α},
    54→        StrictMono g ∧
    55→        ∀ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < target_rest}, i < j → f (g i) (g j) = c)
    56→
    57→/--
    58→Erdős Problem 1171 [Va99, 7.84]:
    59→
    60→Is it true that, for all finite $k < \omega$,
    61→$\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, 3, \ldots, 3)^2_{k+1}$?
    62→
    63→That is, for every $(k+1)$-coloring of pairs of ordinals below $\omega_1^2$, either
    64→there is a homogeneous set of order type $\omega_1 \cdot \omega$ for the first color, or
    65→there is a monochromatic triple for one of the remaining $k$ colors.
    66→
    67→Baumgartner [Ba89b] proved that, assuming a form of Martin's axiom,
    68→$\omega_1 \cdot \omega \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, 3)^2$.
    69→-/
    70→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    71→theorem erdos_1171 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k : ℕ,
    72→    OrdinalPartitionMulticolor (omega1 ^ 2) k (omega1 * omega0) 3 := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos1171
    76→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1172
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1172](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1172)
    23→
    24→Establish whether certain ordinal partition relations hold under the Generalized Continuum
    25→Hypothesis or the Continuum Hypothesis. A problem of Erdős and Hajnal.
    26→
    27→[Va99] Various, _Some of Paul's favorite problems_. Booklet produced for the
    28→conference "Paul Erdős and his mathematics", Budapest (1999).
    29→
    30→[ErRa56] Erdős, P. and Rado, R., _A partition calculus in set theory_.
    31→Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society (1956), 427–489.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Ordinal Cardinal
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos1172
    37→
    38→/-- $\omega_1$, the first uncountable ordinal. -/
    39→noncomputable def omega1 : Ordinal := (aleph 1).ord
    40→
    41→/-- $\omega_2$, the second uncountable ordinal. -/
    42→noncomputable def omega2 : Ordinal := (aleph 2).ord
    43→
    44→/-- $\omega_3$, the third uncountable ordinal. -/
    45→noncomputable def omega3 : Ordinal := (aleph 3).ord
    46→
    47→/-- The ordinal partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, \gamma)^2$ for 2-colorings of pairs.
    48→    For every 2-coloring of the pairs of ordinals below $\alpha$, there is either
    49→    a homogeneous set of order type $\beta$ in color true, or a homogeneous set of
    50→    order type $\gamma$ in color false. Formalized via strictly monotone embeddings:
    51→    a subset of order type $\beta$ corresponds to a strictly monotone function
    52→    from $\{x \mid x < \beta\}$ to $\{x \mid x < \alpha\}$. -/
    53→def OrdinalPartitionPair (α β γ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    54→  ∀ f : {x : Ordinal // x < α} → {x : Ordinal // x < α} → Bool,
    55→    (∃ g : {x : Ordinal // x < β} → {x : Ordinal // x < α},
    56→      StrictMono g ∧
    57→      ∀ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < β}, i < j → f (g i) (g j) = true) ∨
    58→    (∃ g : {x : Ordinal // x < γ} → {x : Ordinal // x < α},
    59→      StrictMono g ∧
    60→      ∀ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < γ}, i < j → f (g i) (g j) = false)
    61→
    62→/-- The Generalized Continuum Hypothesis: $2^{\aleph_o} = \aleph_{o+1}$ for all ordinals $o$. -/
    63→def GCH : Prop := ∀ o : Ordinal.{0}, (2 : Cardinal.{0}) ^ aleph o = aleph (o + 1)
    64→
    65→/-- The Continuum Hypothesis: $2^{\aleph_0} = \aleph_1$. -/
    66→def CH : Prop := (2 : Cardinal.{0}) ^ aleph 0 = aleph 1
    67→
    68→/--
    69→Erdős Problem 1172, Part 1 [Va99, 7.87]:
    70→
    71→Assuming the Generalized Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
    72→$$\omega_3 \to (\omega_2, \omega_1 + 2)^2.$$
    73→
    74→This is an open problem of Erdős and Hajnal. The right-hand side may have
    75→been filled in incorrectly due to a truncated photocopy of the source [Va99].
    76→-/
    77→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    78→theorem erdos_1172 : answer(sorry) ↔ (GCH →
    79→    OrdinalPartitionPair omega3 omega2 (omega1 + 2)) := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→/--
    83→Erdős Problem 1172, Part 2 [Va99, 7.87]:
    84→
    85→Assuming the Generalized Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
    86→$$\omega_3 \to (\omega_2 + \omega_1, \omega_2 + \omega)^2.$$
    87→
    88→This is an open problem of Erdős and Hajnal.
    89→-/
    90→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    91→theorem erdos_1172.variants.gch_partition_2 : answer(sorry) ↔ (GCH →
    92→    OrdinalPartitionPair omega3 (omega2 + omega1) (omega2 + omega0)) := by
    93→  sorry
    94→
    95→/--
    96→Erdős Problem 1172, Part 3 [Va99, 7.87]:
    97→
    98→Assuming the Generalized Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
    99→$$\omega_2 \to (\omega_1^{\omega+2} + 2, \omega_1 + 2)^2.$$
   100→
   101→This is an open problem of Erdős and Hajnal. The right-hand side may have
   102→been filled in incorrectly due to a truncated photocopy of the source [Va99].
   103→-/
   104→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
   105→theorem erdos_1172.variants.gch_partition_3 : answer(sorry) ↔ (GCH →
   106→    OrdinalPartitionPair omega2 (omega1 ^ (omega0 + 2) + 2) (omega1 + 2)) := by
   107→  sorry
   108→
   109→/--
   110→Erdős Problem 1172, Part 4 [Va99, 7.87]:
   111→
   112→Assuming the Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
   113→$$\omega_2 \to (\omega_1 + \omega)^2_2.$$
   114→
   115→That is, for every 2-coloring of the pairs of ordinals below $\omega_2$, there exists
   116→a monochromatic homogeneous set of order type $\omega_1 + \omega$.
   117→
   118→This is an open problem of Erdős and Hajnal.
   119→-/
   120→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
   121→theorem erdos_1172.variants.ch_partition : answer(sorry) ↔ (CH →
   122→    OrdinalPartitionPair omega2 (omega1 + omega0) (omega1 + omega0)) := by
   123→  sorry
   124→
   125→end Erdos1172
   126→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1173
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1173](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1173)
    23→
    24→[Ko25b] Koepke, P., Problem 35.
    25→
    26→[Va99] Vaughan, J.E., _Small uncountable cardinals and topology_. Open Problems in
    27→Topology (1999), §7.88.
    28→
    29→A problem of Erdős and Hajnal.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Ordinal Cardinal
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos1173
    35→
    36→/-- The Generalized Continuum Hypothesis: $2^{\aleph_\alpha} = \aleph_{\alpha+1}$ for all
    37→ordinals $\alpha$. -/
    38→def GCH : Prop := ∀ o : Ordinal.{0}, (2 : Cardinal.{0}) ^ aleph o = aleph (o + 1)
    39→
    40→/-- $\omega_{\omega+1}$, the initial ordinal of $\aleph_{\omega+1}$. -/
    41→noncomputable def omegaOmega1 : Ordinal := (aleph (ω + 1)).ord
    42→
    43→/-- A set $H \subseteq \omega_{\omega+1}$ is free for $f$ if for all $\alpha \in H$,
    44→$f(\alpha) \cap H \subseteq \{\alpha\}$,
    45→i.e., $\alpha \notin f(\beta)$ for all distinct $\alpha, \beta \in H$. -/
    46→def IsFreeSet (f : {α : Ordinal // α < omegaOmega1} → Set {α : Ordinal // α < omegaOmega1})
    47→    (H : Set {α : Ordinal // α < omegaOmega1}) : Prop :=
    48→  ∀ α ∈ H, f α ∩ H ⊆ {α}
    49→
    50→/--
    51→Erdős Problem 1173 [Ko25b, Problem 35] [Va99, 7.88]:
    52→
    53→Assuming the Generalised Continuum Hypothesis, let
    54→$f : \omega_{\omega+1} \to [\omega_{\omega+1}]^{\leq \aleph_\omega}$
    55→be a set mapping such that $|f(\alpha) \cap f(\beta)| < \aleph_\omega$ for all
    56→$\alpha \neq \beta$.
    57→Does there exist a free set of cardinality $\aleph_{\omega+1}$?
    58→
    59→Here $\omega_{\omega+1} = (\aleph_{\omega+1}).\mathrm{ord}$ is the initial ordinal of
    60→$\aleph_{\omega+1}$, elements are ordinals $\alpha < \omega_{\omega+1}$, and a free set $H$
    61→satisfies $f(\alpha) \cap H \subseteq \{\alpha\}$ for all $\alpha \in H$.
    62→The cardinality comparison uses `Cardinal.lift` to reconcile universe levels:
    63→subsets of $\{\alpha : \mathrm{Ordinal} \mid \alpha < \omega_{\omega+1}\}$ live in `Type 1`,
    64→while `aleph` lives in `Cardinal.{0}`; `Cardinal.lift.{1,0}` embeds `Cardinal.{0}` into
    65→`Cardinal.{1}`.
    66→
    67→A problem of Erdős and Hajnal.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    70→theorem erdos_1173 : answer(sorry) ↔
    71→    ∀ (h : GCH)
    72→      (f : {α : Ordinal // α < omegaOmega1} → Set {α : Ordinal // α < omegaOmega1})
    73→      (hf : ∀ α : {x : Ordinal // x < omegaOmega1},
    74→        Cardinal.mk ↥(f α) ≤ Cardinal.lift.{1, 0} (aleph ω))
    75→      (hfI : ∀ α β : {x : Ordinal // x < omegaOmega1}, α ≠ β →
    76→        Cardinal.mk ↥(f α ∩ f β) < Cardinal.lift.{1, 0} (aleph ω)),
    77→    ∃ H : Set {α : Ordinal // α < omegaOmega1},
    78→      IsFreeSet f H ∧ Cardinal.lift.{1, 0} (aleph (ω + 1)) ≤ Cardinal.mk ↥H := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→end Erdos1173
    82→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1174
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1174](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1174)
    23→
    24→A problem of Erdős and Hajnal on graph colourings and cliques.
    25→Shelah proved that a graph with either property can consistently exist.
    26→
    27→[Va99] Vardi, I. (ed.), *Problems from the Erdős problem collection*, §7.91 (1999).
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open Cardinal SimpleGraph
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos1174
    33→
    34→/--
    35→Erdős Problem 1174, Part 1 [Va99, §7.91]:
    36→
    37→Does there exist a graph $G$ with no $K_4$ (no 4-clique) such that for every
    38→edge colouring of $G$ with countably many colours ($\mathbb{N}$), some monochromatic
    39→$K_3$ (a 3-clique whose three edges all receive the same colour) exists in $G$?
    40→
    41→This is an open problem of Erdős and Hajnal. Shelah proved that such a graph
    42→can consistently exist (i.e., its existence is consistent with ZFC).
    43→-/
    44→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    45→theorem erdos_1174 : answer(sorry) ↔
    46→    ∃ (V : Type) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    47→      (∀ S : Finset V, ¬G.IsNClique 4 S) ∧
    48→      ∀ (col : Sym2 V → ℕ),
    49→        ∃ (S : Finset V) (c : ℕ), G.IsNClique 3 S ∧
    50→          ∀ u ∈ S, ∀ v ∈ S, u ≠ v → col s(u, v) = c := by
    51→  sorry
    52→
    53→/--
    54→Erdős Problem 1174, Part 2 [Va99, §7.91]:
    55→
    56→Does there exist a graph $G$ with no $K_{\aleph_1}$ (no clique of cardinality
    57→$\geq \aleph_1$) such that for every edge colouring of $G$ with countably many colours
    58→($\mathbb{N}$), some monochromatic $K_{\aleph_0}$ exists — that is, a clique of cardinality
    59→$\geq \aleph_0$ whose edges all receive the same colour?
    60→
    61→This is an open problem of Erdős and Hajnal. Shelah proved that such a graph
    62→can consistently exist (i.e., its existence is consistent with ZFC).
    63→-/
    64→@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    65→theorem erdos_1174.variants.infinite_clique : answer(sorry) ↔
    66→    ∃ (V : Type) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    67→      (¬∃ S : Set V, aleph 1 ≤ Cardinal.mk ↥S ∧ G.IsClique S) ∧
    68→      ∀ (col : Sym2 V → ℕ),
    69→        ∃ (S : Set V) (c : ℕ), aleph 0 ≤ Cardinal.mk ↥S ∧ G.IsClique S ∧
    70→          ∀ u ∈ S, ∀ v ∈ S, u ≠ v → col s(u, v) = c := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos1174
    74→
